section.title: Beelzebul and the Sign of Jonah
section.sectionRationale: The conflict reaches its crisis. Faced with an undeniable exorcism, the Pharisees credit it to Satan, and Jesus exposes the absurdity and danger of their charge, warning of the unforgivable blasphemy against the Spirit. He teaches that words reveal the heart, refuses their demand for a sign except the sign of Jonah, His resurrection, and redefines His true family as those who do the Father's will.
section.thesis: By the Spirit of God Jesus overthrows Satan's kingdom, binding the strong man, so that to credit His work to the devil is a hardened blasphemy that reveals the heart; He gives no sign but His death and resurrection, warns against empty reform, and gathers a new family of all who do the Father's will.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the charge of Beelzebul answered; the blasphemy against the Spirit; words revealing the heart; the sign of Jonah; the return of the unclean spirit; and the true family of Jesus.

verses.text: Then a demon-possessed man who was blind and mute was brought to Jesus, and He healed the man so that he could speak and see.
The crowds were astounded and asked, “Could this be the Son of David?”
But when the Pharisees heard this, they said, “Only by {{beelzeboul|Beelzebul}}, the prince of demons, does this man drive out demons.”
Knowing their thoughts, Jesus said to them, “Every kingdom divided against itself will be laid waste, and every city or household divided against itself will not stand.
If Satan drives out Satan, he is divided against himself. How then can his kingdom stand?
And if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons drive them out? So then, they will be your judges.
But if I drive out demons by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.
Or again, how can anyone enter a strong man’s house and steal his possessions, unless he first ties up the strong man? Then he can plunder his house.
He who is not with Me is against Me, and he who does not gather with Me scatters.
Therefore I tell you, every sin and {{blasphemia|blasphemy}} will be forgiven men, but the blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven.
Whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, either in this age or in the one to come.

units.label: Words reveal the heart
units.note: out of the overflow of the heart

verses.text: Make a tree good and its fruit will be good, or make a tree bad and its fruit will be bad, for a tree is known by its fruit.
You brood of vipers, how can you who are evil say anything good? For out of the overflow of the {{kardia|heart}} the mouth speaks.
The good man brings good things out of his good store of treasure, and the evil man brings evil things out of his evil store of treasure.
But I tell you that men will give an account on the day of judgment for every careless word they have spoken.
For by your words you will be acquitted, and by your words you will be condemned.”

units.label: The sign of Jonah
units.note: three days in the heart of the earth

verses.text: Then some of the scribes and Pharisees said to Him, “Teacher, we want to see a {{semeion|sign}} from You.”
Jesus replied, “A wicked and adulterous generation demands a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of the prophet Jonah.
For as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.
The men of Nineveh will stand at the judgment with this generation and condemn it; for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and now One greater than Jonah is here.
The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it; for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and now One greater than Solomon is here.
When an unclean spirit comes out of a man, it passes through arid places seeking rest and does not find it.
Then it says, ‘I will return to the house I left.’ On its return, it finds the house vacant, swept clean, and put in order.

Then it goes and brings with it seven other spirits more wicked than itself, and they go in and dwell there. And the final plight of that man is worse than the first. So will it be with this wicked generation.”

units.label: The true family
units.note: whoever does the will of My Father

verses.text: While Jesus was still speaking to the crowds, His mother and brothers stood outside, wanting to speak to Him.
Someone told Him, “Look, Your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to speak to You.”
But Jesus replied, “Who is My mother, and who are My brothers?”
Pointing to His disciples, He said, “Here are My mother and My brothers.
For whoever does the will of My Father in heaven is My brother and sister and mother.”

beelzeboul.gloss: Beelzebul, a name for the prince of demons, that is, Satan. The Pharisees use it to explain away Jesus' exorcism, crediting the Spirit's work to the devil, the very charge Jesus warns is unforgivable when it hardens into settled rejection.

blasphemia.gloss: Blasphemy, slander, defiant speech against God. Every blasphemy can be forgiven except the blasphemy against the Spirit, the deliberate, hardened calling of God's evident work the devil's, which shows a heart closed against the only One who brings repentance.

kardia.gloss: Heart, the inner core of thought, will, and affection. Words are not incidental; out of the overflow of the heart the mouth speaks, so speech is a reliable index of the heart, by which one is finally acquitted or condemned.

semeion.gloss: Sign, a miraculous token meant to authenticate. The leaders demand a sign despite all they have seen; Jesus refuses any but the sign of Jonah, His three days in the earth and resurrection, the decisive proof of His identity.

basic.context: Jesus frees a blind and mute demoniac, and the crowds wonder if He is the Messiah. The Pharisees, unable to deny the miracle, claim He casts out demons by Satan. Jesus shows how absurd that is, a kingdom cannot war against itself, and reveals that He acts by the Spirit of God, having bound the strong man. He warns of the unforgivable blasphemy against the Spirit, teaches that words reveal the heart, refuses a sign except His resurrection, and names His true family as those who do God's will.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the turning point where opposition curdles into blasphemy. Jesus' logic about the divided kingdom and the strong man interprets His exorcisms as the invasion of Satan's realm by God's reign. The warning about blaspheming the Spirit must be read in context: it is the hardened, deliberate rejection the Pharisees model, not an accidental word. The sign of Jonah points ahead to the cross and resurrection.
basic.originalLanguages: The Pharisees credit Jesus' power to Beelzebul, the prince of demons. Jesus warns of blasphemy against the Spirit, a defiant rejection that cannot be forgiven while it stands. He teaches that words flow from the heart, the inner self, and so reveal it. And He refuses their demand for a sign, giving only the sign of Jonah, His death and resurrection.
basic.typology: Jonah's three days in the fish foreshadow Jesus' three days in the tomb and His rising. The men of Nineveh who repented and the Queen of the South who sought Solomon's wisdom foreshadow the outsiders who respond while the privileged reject. And binding the strong man fulfills the promised overthrow of the powers of darkness by God's reign.
basic.christ: Jesus is the stronger one who binds Satan and plunders his house, the bringer of God's kingdom by the Spirit. He is greater than Jonah and greater than Solomon, prophet and king in one. And the sign He gives is His own resurrection, the heart of the gospel, by which He is vindicated as the Son of Man.
basic.god: God's kingdom is advancing by His Spirit, overthrowing the reign of evil in the ministry of Jesus. God will hold people accountable for their words, which reveal their hearts. And He is forming a new family defined not by blood but by doing His will, gathered around His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Jonah 1 and 2 give the three days in the fish. 1 Kings 10 tells of the Queen of Sheba seeking Solomon. Isaiah 49 speaks of dividing the spoil of the strong. And Hebrews 6 warns against a hardened falling away that cannot be renewed to repentance.
basic.oneStory: At the crisis of the conflict, Jesus reveals that His exorcisms are the in-breaking of God's kingdom, binding the strong man. To call this the devil's work is a hardening blasphemy that seals the heart. Jesus gives no sign but His resurrection, warns that outsiders who repent will condemn the privileged who reject Him, and gathers a new family of all who do the Father's will, the family the whole story has been forming.

context.literary: This section climaxes the growing hostility of chapter 12 and prepares for the parables of chapter 13, where Jesus begins to teach in parables because of this rejection. The Beelzebul controversy, the sign of Jonah, and the true-family scene each contrast hardened opposition with true response, and the empty-house parable warns that mere reform without Christ ends worse.
context.historical: Jewish exorcists existed, which is why Jesus asks by whom their sons cast out demons. Beelzebul was a mocking name for Satan. Demanding a sign despite abundant miracles reflects willful unbelief. Nineveh's repentance under Jonah and the Queen of Sheba's journey to Solomon were well known; that pagans would condemn Israel was a stinging reversal.
context.canonical: The sign of Jonah connects the resurrection to the Old Testament, Jonah 1:17, and becomes a key apologetic for Jesus' vindication. Binding the strong man draws on Isaiah 49:24-25. The warning about blaspheming the Spirit and the empty house resonate with the New Testament's sober warnings against apostasy, Hebrews 6:4-6, and the redefinition of family around obedience anticipates the church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that Jesus overthrows Satan's kingdom by the Spirit of God, to warn that crediting this to the devil is a hardened, unforgivable blasphemy, to point to the resurrection as the decisive sign, and to redefine Jesus' true family as those who do the Father's will.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The controversy describes the crisis of unbelief and Jesus' response. The warnings about words and about blaspheming the Spirit are prescriptive and sobering. The empty-house parable teaches a real danger. The redefinition of family sets the abiding principle that kinship with Jesus comes through doing the Father's will, not physical relation.

debates.question: What is the blasphemy against the Spirit that will not be forgiven?

views.label: The hardened, deliberate attribution of the Spirit's work to Satan
views.case: In context it is exactly what the Pharisees do: seeing God's evident work through the Spirit and knowingly calling it demonic, a settled defiance that closes the heart to the only source of repentance.

views.label: A persistent, final rejection rather than one utterance
views.case: The unforgivable sin is not an isolated word but a fixed, ongoing refusal of the Spirit's testimony to Christ; those anxious that they have committed it show, by their very concern, a heart still tender.

debates.bearing: Both offer comfort to the troubled and warning to the hardened: the sin is a resolute, ongoing rejection of the Spirit's witness, not a slip of the tongue, and the willing sinner who turns to Christ is never turned away.

debates.question: How is the sign of Jonah fulfilled, and how is the three days reckoned?

views.label: The resurrection after burial
views.case: The sign is Jesus' death, burial, and rising; as Jonah emerged from the fish, so Jesus rises from the tomb, the decisive vindication offered to an unbelieving generation.

views.label: Three days and nights by inclusive reckoning
views.case: Jewish counting treated any part of a day as a day, so Friday to Sunday spans three days; the phrase heart of the earth need not require three full 24-hour periods.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that the one sign Jesus gives is His resurrection, the ground of Christian faith, foreshadowed by Jonah and proving Him greater than every prophet and king.

study.originalLanguages: The Pharisees credit Jesus' exorcism to Beelzeboul, the prince of demons; Jesus shows that if He casts out demons by the Spirit of God, then God's kingdom has come, for He has bound the strong man to plunder his house. He warns of blasphemia against the Spirit, a defiant slander of God's evident work that cannot be forgiven while it stands. Speech, He teaches, flows from the kardia, the heart, and so reveals it, acquitting or condemning. And to those demanding a semeion, a sign, He gives only the sign of Jonah, His three days in the earth and His rising, the proof that One greater than Jonah and Solomon is here.

typology.fulfillment: The three days point to Jesus' burial and resurrection, the sign given to an unbelieving generation, Jonah 1:17.
Jesus overpowers Satan to free his captives, fulfilling the promise that the prey of the mighty will be delivered, Isaiah 49:24-25.
Nineveh and the Queen of the South foreshadow the Gentiles who respond to Christ while the privileged reject Him.

study.christ: Jesus is revealed here as the stronger one who has invaded the domain of darkness. His exorcisms are no alliance with Satan but the plundering of Satan's house, for He has bound the strong man by the Spirit of God, and where He acts the kingdom of God has come. He is greater than Jonah the prophet and greater than Solomon the king, and the only sign He will give a demanding generation is the greatest of all: His three days in the heart of the earth and His resurrection. In refusing every lesser proof and pointing to the empty tomb, Jesus stakes His identity on the cross and the rising that will vindicate Him as the Son of Man and the hope of the world.
study.god: God's reign is breaking into the world through the Spirit-empowered ministry of His Son, overthrowing the tyranny of evil. This God takes words with utter seriousness, for they reveal the heart, and He will judge every careless one. He warns solemnly that persistent, defiant rejection of His Spirit's witness closes the door to forgiveness, not because His mercy is small but because such a heart refuses the only means of repentance. And He is gathering a new family, bound not by blood but by glad obedience to His will, a family with room for all who come to His Son.

crossReferences.note: Jonah three days and nights in the great fish, the sign Jesus applies to His burial and resurrection.
The Queen of Sheba seeking Solomon's wisdom, a pagan who will condemn the unbelieving generation.
The captives of the mighty will be freed, the background to binding the strong man.
A sober warning against a hardened falling away, resonant with the blasphemy against the Spirit.

study.oneStory: The conflict comes to a head as Jesus' enemies call the work of the Spirit the work of the devil. Jesus answers that His power over demons is the arrival of God's kingdom, the binding of the strong man, and He warns that such hardened blasphemy seals the heart. He gives no sign but His resurrection, foretells that repentant outsiders will condemn the privileged, and gathers a new family of all who do the Father's will. The story moves toward the tomb where the sign of Jonah will be fulfilled and the kingdom secured.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer the charge that He cast out demons by Beelzebul?

options.text: A kingdom divided against itself cannot stand; He acts by the Spirit of God, having bound the strong man.
options.note: Verses 25-29 expose the absurdity and reveal the true source of His power.

options.text: He admitted the source of His power was uncertain.
options.note: He was clear it was the Spirit of God, not uncertain, and He exposed their charge as absurd.

options.text: He refused to respond to the accusation.
options.note: He answered at length, showing the charge was self-defeating.

options.text: He agreed that some of His power came from Satan.
options.note: He decisively denied it, attributing His work to the Spirit of God.

quiz.q: What is the blasphemy against the Spirit that will not be forgiven?

options.text: The hardened, deliberate rejection that calls the Spirit's evident work the devil's, as the Pharisees did.
options.note: Verses 31-32 warn of this settled defiance against the Spirit's witness.

options.text: Any single angry word spoken against God in a weak moment.
options.note: It is a settled, defiant rejection, not an isolated word; this understates the hardness involved.

options.text: Speaking a word against the Son of Man.
options.note: Jesus says that can be forgiven; the unforgivable sin is against the Spirit's clear testimony.

options.text: Failing to perform enough religious duties.
options.note: It concerns hardened rejection of the Spirit, not a shortage of religious works.

quiz.q: What did Jesus teach about our words?

options.text: Words flow from the heart and reveal it, so we are acquitted or condemned by them.
options.note: Verses 34-37 tie speech to the overflow of the heart and to judgment.

options.text: Words are unimportant as long as actions are good.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus says even careless words will be accounted for because they reveal the heart.

options.text: Only public speeches, not private words, matter to God.
options.note: He warns of every careless word, not only public speech.

options.text: God pays no attention to what people say.
options.note: Jesus says we give account for our words on the day of judgment.

quiz.q: What sign did Jesus promise the sign-seeking generation?

options.text: Only the sign of Jonah, His three days in the earth and His resurrection.
options.note: Verses 39-40 give the sign of Jonah as pointing to His burial and rising.

options.text: A dramatic display of fire from heaven.
options.note: He refused spectacular signs, giving only the sign of Jonah, His resurrection.

options.text: No sign of any kind whatsoever.
options.note: He did promise one sign, the sign of Jonah, though He refused their demand for a spectacle.

options.text: The immediate overthrow of Rome.
options.note: The sign was His resurrection, not political conquest.

quiz.q: Who did Jesus say are His true family?

options.text: Whoever does the will of His Father in heaven is His brother, sister, and mother.
options.note: Verses 48-50 redefine family around doing the Father's will.

options.text: Only His biological mother and brothers.
options.note: He pointed beyond biological ties to those who do the Father's will.

options.text: Only the twelve apostles, and no others.
options.note: He included all who do the Father's will, not only the twelve.

options.text: Anyone who admires Him from a distance.
options.note: True kinship is doing the Father's will, not mere admiration.

questions: The Pharisees saw undeniable good and called it evil to protect themselves. Where are you tempted to explain away God's work because acknowledging it would cost you something?
Jesus said words flow from the heart. What do your habitual words reveal about the state of your heart, and where do you need God to change it?
The only sign Jesus gave was His resurrection. How does the historical reality of the empty tomb anchor your faith when you are tempted to want other proofs?
The empty, swept house ended worse than before because it was left vacant. How are you not just removing sin but filling your life with Christ and His Spirit?
Jesus welcomes into His family all who do the Father's will. What does it mean to you that obedience to God makes you a brother or sister of Jesus Himself?

translationNotes.note: Beelzebul is a name for the prince of demons, that is, Satan; the Pharisees credit the Spirit's work to him, the charge Jesus warns against.
Three days and three nights follows Jewish inclusive reckoning, in which any part of a day counts as a day; the sign points to Jesus' burial and resurrection.
Some early manuscripts do not include verse 47, as the BSB footnote notes; the sense continues unbroken into Jesus' reply about His true family.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 12:22-50. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
